items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.

items.q: Why do designers often use 5th to 95th percentile data instead of just the 50th?
items.correct: To accommodate the majority of users rather than just the average.
items.misconception: Because the 50th percentile is too unreliable.
items.why: Students think designing for the average user is best, but it excludes most people. The 5th-95th range covers 90% of users - designing only for the mean fails everyone above or below it.

items.q: How many principles of good design did Dieter Rams articulate?
items.why: Correct answer: 10. “12” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which Apple designer cited Rams as a major influence?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Jonathan Ive. “Steve Wozniak” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
